name: Austria
tagline: From the Eastern March to the heart of an empire, and from collapse to a small neutral republic.
summary: Austria's history is the history of a frontier that became the centre of Europe. The name first appears in 996 as 'Ostarrîchi', the 'eastern realm', a march of the Holy Roman Empire ruled by the Babenberg dynasty, who in 1156 were raised to dukes and made Vienna their seat. When the Babenberg line died out, the Habsburgs seized the duchy after Rudolf I's victory at the Marchfeld in 1278, beginning a dynasty that would rule for more than six centuries and turn Vienna into an imperial capital. The Habsburgs held the line against two great Ottoman sieges, in 1529 and 1683, ruled the Holy Roman Empire for generations, and under Maria Theresa reformed their sprawling possessions. In 1804 they proclaimed the Empire of Austria, and after 1867 governed it jointly with Hungary as a multi-ethnic Dual Monarchy. The assassination of the heir Franz Ferdinand at Sarajevo in 1914 helped ignite the First World War, and defeat dissolved Austria-Hungary in 1918. A small German-speaking republic was left behind, its borders fixed by the Treaty of Saint-Germain (1919), which forbade union with Germany and ceded South Tyrol to Italy. Hitler annexed Austria in the Anschluss of 1938; after the Second World War and a decade of four-power occupation, the Austrian State Treaty of 1955 restored full sovereignty, and Austria declared its permanent neutrality. It joined the European Union in 1995.

founding.label: Ostarrîchi: the first mention of Austria
founding.detail: The conventional origin of Austria is dated to 1 November 996, when a charter of Emperor Otto III used the word 'Ostarrîchi', the 'eastern realm', the earliest known form of the German name Österreich. It referred to the marchia orientalis, the Eastern March of the Holy Roman Empire entrusted in 976 to the Bavarian house of Babenberg. Under the Babenbergs the march grew into a coherent principality; in 1156 the Privilegium Minus of Emperor Frederick I Barbarossa elevated Austria to a duchy and detached it from Bavaria, and Henry II 'Jasomirgott' made Vienna his residence. When the male Babenberg line ended in 1246, the duchy passed, after a Bohemian interlude, to the Habsburgs in 1278, opening the dynasty that would shape Austria and much of Europe until 1918.

quickFacts.label: First mention of the name
quickFacts.value: 'Ostarrîchi' in a charter of 1 November 996 (origin of Österreich)

quickFacts.label: Raised to a duchy
quickFacts.value: Privilegium Minus of 1156, under the Babenberg dynasty

quickFacts.label: Habsburg rule begins
quickFacts.value: Rudolf I takes the Austrian lands after the Battle of the Marchfeld, 1278

quickFacts.label: Sovereignty restored & neutrality
quickFacts.value: Austrian State Treaty, 15 May 1955; permanent neutrality, 26 October 1955

eras.title: Ostarrîchi: The Babenberg March and Duchy
eras.standfirst: A frontier march on the Danube, granted to the Babenbergs, acquired the name of Austria, grew into a duchy and chose Vienna as its seat.
body: In 976 the Emperor entrusted the marchia orientalis, the Eastern March of the Holy Roman Empire on the middle Danube, to Count Liutpold of the Bavarian house of Babenberg. The march was a frontier zone facing Hungary and the Slavic east, and it is in connection with these lands that the name of Austria first appears: a charter of Emperor Otto III dated 1 November 996 uses the word 'Ostarrîchi', the 'eastern realm', the earliest known ancestor of the German Österreich.

Over the following century and a half the Babenbergs turned a fragile border command into a settled principality. Margrave Leopold III, later canonised and made patron saint of Austria, anchored the dynasty with monastic foundations such as Klosterneuburg. The decisive constitutional step came in 1156, when the Privilegium Minus issued by Emperor Frederick I Barbarossa raised Austria from a march to a duchy and separated it from Bavaria, granting the Babenbergs unusually wide hereditary rights. Duke Henry II 'Jasomirgott' moved his court to Vienna, which from then on remained the centre of power.

The Babenberg duchy expanded down the Danube and, by purchase and inheritance, acquired Styria in 1192. But the line was running out. In 1246 Duke Frederick II 'the Quarrelsome' was killed in battle against the Hungarians, leaving no male heir and ending some 270 years of Babenberg rule. The vacant duchy became a prize fought over by neighbouring princes, setting the stage for the rise of a new dynasty.

events.title: The Eastern March granted to the Babenbergs
events.summary: The marchia orientalis, a frontier march of the Holy Roman Empire on the Danube, was entrusted to Count Liutpold of the Bavarian house of Babenberg, beginning their long rule over the future Austria.

events.title: First mention of 'Ostarrîchi'
events.summary: A charter of Emperor Otto III used the word 'Ostarrîchi', the 'eastern realm', the earliest recorded form of the name Österreich (Austria), referring to the Babenberg march.

events.title: The Privilegium Minus raises Austria to a duchy
events.summary: Emperor Frederick I Barbarossa's Privilegium Minus elevated Austria from a march to a duchy and detached it from Bavaria, granting the Babenbergs wide hereditary rights; Vienna became their seat.

events.title: End of the Babenberg line
events.summary: Duke Frederick II 'the Quarrelsome' was killed fighting the Hungarians, leaving no male heir; the extinction of the Babenbergs left the duchy of Austria contested among neighbouring powers.

figures.name: Leopold III
figures.blurb: The most revered of the Babenberg margraves, Leopold III consolidated the dynasty and founded religious houses such as Klosterneuburg. He was later canonised and became the patron saint of Austria.

figures.name: Henry II 'Jasomirgott'
figures.blurb: Under the Privilegium Minus of 1156 Henry II became the first duke of an Austria raised above the rank of a march. He moved his residence to Vienna, fixing the city as the centre of Austrian power.

pullquote.text: The name 'Ostarrîchi', the eastern realm, first appears in a charter of 996, and would in time become Österreich, the German name of Austria.
pullquote.attribution: On the origin of the name of Austria

eras.title: The Rise of the Habsburgs and the Ottoman Wars
eras.standfirst: A new dynasty seized the duchy at the Marchfeld, made Vienna an imperial capital, and held the line against two great Ottoman sieges.
body: The contest for the leaderless duchy was decided by force. Rudolf of Habsburg, elected Roman-German king in 1273, defeated the powerful Bohemian king Ottokar II Přemysl at the Battle of the Marchfeld near Dürnkrut on 26 August 1278, where Ottokar was killed. Rudolf occupied the Austrian lands and in 1282 enfeoffed his sons with the duchies of Austria and Styria. This is conventionally regarded as the birth of Habsburg rule in Austria, a dominion that would last until 1918, though contemporaries could hardly have guessed at the dynasty's future greatness.

Over the following centuries the Habsburgs accumulated crowns and titles, repeatedly holding the office of Holy Roman Emperor and making Vienna one of the chief residences of Christian Europe. Their lands lay directly in the path of Ottoman expansion. In the autumn of 1529 the army of Sultan Suleiman the Magnificent besieged Vienna for three weeks before withdrawing as winter approached, the first of the great Turkish assaults on the city. It was a foretaste of more than a century and a half of frontier war.

The second and most famous siege came in 1683, when the Grand Vizier Kara Mustafa led an enormous Ottoman army to the walls of Vienna; the Emperor Leopold I fled the city. On 12 September 1683 a relief army under Charles V, Duke of Lorraine, and King Jan III Sobieski of Poland routed the besiegers in one of the decisive battles of European history. The victory proved the turning point in the Habsburg-Ottoman struggle: a Holy League was formed in 1684, and commanders such as Prince Eugene of Savoy drove the Ottomans out of Hungary, vastly enlarging Habsburg power in the southeast.

events.title: Battle of the Marchfeld
events.summary: Rudolf I of Habsburg defeated and killed the Bohemian king Ottokar II near Dürnkrut on the Marchfeld, seizing the Austrian lands; in 1282 he enfeoffed his sons with them, founding Habsburg rule in Austria.

events.title: First Ottoman siege of Vienna
events.summary: The army of Sultan Suleiman the Magnificent besieged Vienna for three weeks in the autumn of 1529 before withdrawing, the first great Ottoman assault on the Habsburg capital.

events.title: Second Ottoman siege and the relief of Vienna
events.summary: An Ottoman army under Grand Vizier Kara Mustafa besieged Vienna until a relief force led by Charles V of Lorraine and King Jan III Sobieski of Poland routed it on 12 September 1683, a turning point against Ottoman expansion.

events.title: Prince Eugene and the conquest of Hungary
events.summary: After Vienna's deliverance, Prince Eugene of Savoy won victories such as Zenta (1697) and the capture of Belgrade (1717); the Peace of Karlowitz (1699) secured Habsburg control of Hungary and Transylvania.

figures.name: Rudolf I of Habsburg
figures.role: Roman-German king, founder of Habsburg Austria
figures.blurb: Elected king in 1273, Rudolf defeated Ottokar II at the Marchfeld in 1278 and granted the Austrian lands to his sons, beginning more than six centuries of Habsburg rule over Austria.

figures.name: Prince Eugene of Savoy
figures.blurb: The foremost commander of the Habsburg armies, Eugene crushed the Ottomans at Zenta in 1697 and took Belgrade in 1717, driving the Turks from Hungary and earning a place at the heart of Austrian national memory.

pullquote.text: The relief of Vienna on 12 September 1683 proved the turning point in the long struggle between the Habsburgs and the Ottoman Empire.
pullquote.attribution: On the second siege of Vienna

eras.title: Maria Theresa, the Empire of Austria and the Holy Roman Empire
eras.standfirst: A reforming empress held the dynasty together, and her descendants exchanged the Holy Roman crown for a new imperial title of their own.
body: In 1740 the Habsburg inheritance passed, under the Pragmatic Sanction of 1713, to the twenty-three-year-old Maria Theresa, the only woman to rule the Habsburg dominions in her own right. Several powers refused to recognise her, triggering the War of the Austrian Succession (1740-1748), in which she lost the wealthy province of Silesia to Prussia. To strengthen the state she launched a sweeping reform of administration, taxation, the army and schooling, work continued by her son Joseph II. Her marriage to Francis Stephen of Lorraine, who became Holy Roman Emperor in 1745, founded the house of Habsburg-Lorraine; her many children included Joseph II and Marie Antoinette.

The Napoleonic era recast the dynasty's titles. When Napoleon proclaimed himself Emperor of the French in 1804, the Habsburg ruler Franz II, fearing the loss of imperial rank, issued a patent on 11 August 1804 assuming the new hereditary title of Emperor of Austria as Franz I. Two years later, on 6 August 1806, he laid down the crown of the Holy Roman Empire, which was then dissolved after roughly a thousand years. The Habsburg lands were now formally a single Austrian Empire.

After Napoleon's defeat, Vienna became the stage for the great reordering of Europe at the Congress of Vienna in 1814-1815, presided over in spirit by the Austrian statesman Klemens von Metternich. The decades that followed, the 'Vormärz', were an age of conservative restoration and tightening control, until the revolutionary wave of 1848 swept across the empire, forced Metternich from office and brought the young Franz Joseph to the throne.

events.title: Reign of Maria Theresa
events.summary: Inheriting the Habsburg lands under the Pragmatic Sanction, Maria Theresa survived the War of the Austrian Succession and reformed the administration, army and education across her dominions.

events.title: The Empire of Austria proclaimed
events.summary: In response to Napoleon's imperial coronation, Franz II issued a patent assuming the hereditary title of Emperor of Austria as Franz I, formally constituting the Habsburg lands as an empire.

events.title: Dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire
events.summary: Franz II laid down the crown of the Holy Roman Empire, which was dissolved after some thousand years, leaving the Habsburg ruler as Emperor of Austria alone.

events.title: Revolution and the accession of Franz Joseph
events.summary: The revolutions of 1848 shook the Austrian Empire, drove the chancellor Metternich into exile and brought the eighteen-year-old Franz Joseph to the throne, where he would remain until 1916.

figures.name: Maria Theresa
figures.blurb: The only female sovereign of the Habsburg lands, Maria Theresa ruled from 1740 to 1780 as archduchess of Austria and queen of Hungary and Bohemia, modernising the state and founding the house of Habsburg-Lorraine.

figures.name: Franz II/I
figures.role: Last Holy Roman Emperor, first Emperor of Austria
figures.blurb: Franz proclaimed the Empire of Austria in 1804 and in 1806 dissolved the Holy Roman Empire, ending a millennial institution and transforming the Habsburg realm into a single hereditary empire.

pullquote.text: On 11 August 1804 the Habsburg ruler took the title Emperor of Austria; two years later the thousand-year-old Holy Roman Empire was dissolved.
pullquote.attribution: On the founding of the Austrian Empire

eras.title: The Dual Monarchy and the Catastrophe of 1918
eras.standfirst: A multi-ethnic empire reorganised itself as Austria-Hungary, until a shot at Sarajevo helped unleash a war that destroyed it.
body: Franz Joseph reigned for sixty-eight years, from 1848 to 1916, the longest of any Habsburg, over an empire of many nations: Germans, Hungarians, Czechs, Poles, Croats, Slovenes, Romanians, Italians and more. After defeat by Prussia in 1866, pressure from the Hungarian elite forced a fundamental reform. The Compromise (Ausgleich) of 1867 split the realm into two halves, the Austrian lands (Cisleithania) and the Kingdom of Hungary (Transleithania), each with its own parliament and government but united in the person of the Emperor-King and through common ministries of foreign affairs, finance and defence. On 8 June 1867 Franz Joseph was crowned king of Hungary, and the Dual Monarchy of Austria-Hungary was born.

Vienna in these decades was one of the great capitals of the world, its Ringstrasse, music, science and intellectual life giving the empire a brilliant late flowering even as nationalist tensions strained the multi-ethnic state from within. The historic centre of the city, with its medieval, Baroque and nineteenth-century 'Gründerzeit' layers, would later be recognised by UNESCO as a World Heritage site for its role as the political and cultural centre of this multinational empire.

The end came suddenly. On 28 June 1914 the heir to the throne, Archduke Franz Ferdinand, and his wife Sophie were shot dead in Sarajevo by the young Bosnian Serb Gavrilo Princip. After the July Crisis, Austria-Hungary declared war on Serbia on 28 July 1914, drawing the great powers into the First World War. Four years of war exhausted and fractured the empire. Franz Joseph died in 1916; his successor Karl I could not hold the realm together. As the war was lost, the subject nations broke away, Karl renounced participation in government on 11 November 1918, and on 12 November the Republic of German-Austria was proclaimed, ending some 640 years of Habsburg rule.

events.title: The Austro-Hungarian Compromise
events.summary: The Ausgleich of 1867 reorganised the empire into the Dual Monarchy of Austria-Hungary, two states sharing a common monarch, foreign policy, finance and army; Franz Joseph was crowned king of Hungary on 8 June.

events.title: Assassination at Sarajevo
events.summary: Archduke Franz Ferdinand, heir to the throne, and his wife Sophie were assassinated in Sarajevo by Gavrilo Princip, the spark that led Austria-Hungary to war.

events.title: Austria-Hungary declares war on Serbia
events.summary: After the July Crisis, Austria-Hungary declared war on Serbia, triggering a chain of alliances that plunged Europe into the First World War.

events.title: Collapse of Austria-Hungary and the First Republic
events.summary: As the war was lost the empire disintegrated; Emperor Karl I renounced participation in government on 11 November and the Republic of German-Austria was proclaimed on 12 November 1918, ending Habsburg rule.

figures.name: Franz Joseph I
figures.role: Emperor of Austria, King of Hungary
figures.blurb: Ruling from 1848 to 1916, Franz Joseph presided over the creation of Austria-Hungary in 1867 and over a multi-ethnic empire whose tensions and the war that began in 1914 he did not live to see resolved.

figures.name: Franz Ferdinand
figures.role: Archduke and heir to the throne
figures.blurb: Heir to Franz Joseph, Franz Ferdinand was assassinated with his wife Sophie at Sarajevo on 28 June 1914 by Gavrilo Princip, the event that precipitated the outbreak of the First World War.

pullquote.text: The assassination at Sarajevo on 28 June 1914 served as the pretext for the war that within four years destroyed Austria-Hungary altogether.
pullquote.attribution: On the end of the Dual Monarchy

eras.title: The First Republic, Saint-Germain and the Anschluss
eras.standfirst: A small republic, its borders dictated and union with Germany forbidden, was nonetheless annexed by Hitler and dragged into the Second World War.
body: The republic that emerged from the ruins of empire was a small, German-speaking state that many of its own citizens regarded as too small to survive. Its borders and status were fixed by the victors in the Treaty of Saint-Germain, signed for Austria by Chancellor Karl Renner on 10 September 1919. The treaty confirmed the dissolution of the Habsburg empire, assigned Austria 'sole war guilt', limited its army and, crucially, forbade union (Anschluss) with Germany without the consent of the League of Nations, even barring the name 'German-Austria'.

Among the treaty's most contested provisions was the cession of South Tyrol, the German-speaking southern part of the historic crown land of Tyrol, to the Kingdom of Italy. The transfer placed a large German-speaking population under Italian rule and created a grievance that would shape Austrian and Italian politics for decades. The First Republic itself was unstable, riven between Social Democrats and Christian Socials, and slid in 1933-1934 into an authoritarian 'corporate state' under Engelbert Dollfuss, who was murdered by Austrian Nazis in 1934.

On 12 March 1938 German troops crossed the border, and on 13 March the Nazi chancellor Arthur Seyss-Inquart signed a law declaring the reunification of Austria with the German Reich; the Anschluss extinguished Austrian independence. A plebiscite in April was manipulated to record near-unanimous approval. As part of the Reich, Austria was drawn into the Second World War and into the persecution and murder of its Jews; Vienna's large Jewish community was devastated. Liberation came only with Germany's defeat in 1945.

events.title: Treaty of Saint-Germain
events.summary: Signed for Austria by Karl Renner, the treaty confirmed the breakup of Austria-Hungary, forbade union with Germany without League of Nations approval, limited Austria's army and ceded South Tyrol to Italy.

events.title: South Tyrol ceded to Italy
events.summary: Under Saint-Germain the German-speaking southern part of historic Tyrol was awarded to Italy, placing a large German-speaking population under Italian rule and creating a lasting territorial grievance.

events.title: Dollfuss and the corporate state
events.summary: Chancellor Engelbert Dollfuss suspended parliament and established an authoritarian 'corporate state'; he was assassinated by Austrian Nazis in July 1934, deepening the First Republic's crisis.

events.title: The Anschluss
events.summary: German troops entered Austria on 12 March 1938 and on 13 March a law proclaimed reunification with the Reich; Austrian independence ended and a manipulated April plebiscite recorded near-total approval.

figures.name: Karl Renner
figures.blurb: A Social Democratic statesman, Renner headed the first government of the republic and signed the Treaty of Saint-Germain in 1919; he returned to lead Austria's provisional government in 1945 and became its first post-war president.

figures.name: Engelbert Dollfuss
figures.blurb: Dollfuss dismantled parliamentary democracy and built an authoritarian Catholic 'corporate state' in 1933-1934, banning rival parties. He was assassinated by Austrian Nazis in a failed coup in July 1934.

pullquote.text: The Treaty of Saint-Germain forbade Austria's union with Germany and handed German-speaking South Tyrol to Italy, fixing grievances that long outlasted the First Republic.

eras.title: The Second Republic: Neutrality, South Tyrol and Europe
eras.standfirst: Occupied by four powers for a decade, Austria regained sovereignty in 1955, declared permanent neutrality, settled the South Tyrol question and joined the European Union.
body: After 1945 Austria, like Germany, was divided into four occupation zones administered by the United States, the United Kingdom, France and the Soviet Union, with Vienna under joint control. A re-established republic governed under Allied supervision while the powers negotiated its future. The breakthrough came in 1955: in exchange for a declaration of neutrality, the Soviet Union agreed to withdraw. The Austrian State Treaty was signed at the Belvedere Palace in Vienna on 15 May 1955 by the four occupying powers and the Austrian foreign minister Leopold Figl, restoring full sovereignty; it came into force on 27 July, the last occupation troops departed in October, and on 26 October 1955 parliament enacted a constitutional law declaring Austria's permanent neutrality. Austria joined the United Nations in December 1955.

The fate of South Tyrol remained unresolved. The Gruber-De Gasperi Agreement of 5 September 1946, signed by the Austrian foreign minister Karl Gruber and the Italian prime minister Alcide De Gasperi, committed Italy to grant the German-speaking population autonomy and equality of language and education. The first autonomy statute of 1948, however, diluted these guarantees within a wider region, and discontent grew. Austria brought the dispute before the United Nations, whose General Assembly adopted Resolution 1497 (XV) on 31 October 1960 and Resolution 1661 (XVI) on 28 November 1961, calling on the two states to negotiate.

Years of talks produced the 'Package' of 137 measures, approved in 1969, and a Second Autonomy Statute that entered into force on 20 January 1972, transferring real powers to the province of Bolzano and protecting its German-speaking and Ladin communities; Austria and Italy formally closed the dispute in 1992. At home, Austria built a prosperous social-market democracy, long associated with the chancellorship of Bruno Kreisky. After the end of the Cold War, Austrians voted in a referendum on 12 June 1994 to join the European Union, and the country became a member on 1 January 1995, while retaining its neutrality.

events.title: The Austrian State Treaty
events.summary: Signed at the Belvedere in Vienna by the four occupying powers and foreign minister Leopold Figl, the treaty restored Austria's full sovereignty and ended the four-power occupation; it came into force on 27 July 1955.

events.title: Declaration of permanent neutrality
events.summary: After the last occupation troops left, the Austrian parliament passed a constitutional law declaring the republic's permanent neutrality, the condition on which the Soviet Union had agreed to withdraw.

events.title: Gruber-De Gasperi Agreement on South Tyrol
events.summary: Austrian foreign minister Karl Gruber and Italian premier Alcide De Gasperi signed an accord committing Italy to grant autonomy and language and education rights to South Tyrol's German-speaking population.

events.title: South Tyrol's Second Autonomy Statute
events.summary: After the dispute reached the UN (Resolutions 1497 of 1960 and 1661 of 1961) and the 'Package' of 1969, the Second Autonomy Statute took effect in 1972, granting real autonomy to the province of Bolzano.

events.title: Accession to the European Union
events.summary: Following a referendum on 12 June 1994 in which about two-thirds voted in favour, Austria joined the European Union on 1 January 1995, integrating the neutral republic into the European mainstream.

figures.name: Leopold Figl
figures.blurb: A survivor of Nazi concentration camps and a leading figure of the Second Republic, Figl signed the Austrian State Treaty for Austria in 1955 and is remembered for proclaiming 'Austria is free!' on its conclusion.

figures.name: Karl Gruber
figures.blurb: As foreign minister of the new republic, Gruber negotiated the 1946 agreement with the Italian premier De Gasperi that committed Italy to autonomy for German-speaking South Tyrol, the basis of the later settlement.

pullquote.text: On 26 October 1955 Austria declared its permanent neutrality of its own free will, the condition on which it had recovered full sovereignty months earlier.
pullquote.attribution: On the State Treaty and neutrality

figures.name: Rudolf I of Habsburg
figures.blurb: Roman-German king from 1273, Rudolf seized the Austrian lands after his victory at the Marchfeld in 1278, founding the Habsburg dynasty that ruled Austria for more than six centuries.

figures.name: Maria Theresa
figures.blurb: The only woman to rule the Habsburg lands in her own right, Maria Theresa reigned from 1740 to 1780, reformed the state and army, and founded the house of Habsburg-Lorraine.

figures.name: Franz Joseph I
figures.role: Emperor of Austria, King of Hungary
figures.blurb: Reigning for sixty-eight years, Franz Joseph created the Dual Monarchy of Austria-Hungary in 1867 and ruled the multi-ethnic empire until his death in the midst of the First World War.

figures.name: Leopold Figl
figures.blurb: A central figure of the Second Republic, Figl signed the Austrian State Treaty of 1955 that restored Austria's sovereignty after a decade of four-power occupation.
